intro: Frontend system design interviews ask you to architect **large-scale web applications**: data flow, caching, performance, real-time features, and trade-offs. Use the sections below to study concepts before drilling the practice prompts.

sections.title: How to answer a system design question
points: Clarify requirements: functional, scale (users, data), latency, consistency.
Sketch high-level: client → CDN / edge → API / BFF → services → data stores.
Deep-dive on 2–3 areas: e.g. state, caching, real-time, or media.
Call out trade-offs: consistency vs availability, complexity vs speed to ship.
Discuss failure modes: retries, idempotency, graceful degradation.

sections.title: CAP & consistency (know the basics)
points: **C**onsistency, **A**vailability, **P**artition tolerance — in a network partition you often choose between C and A.
Strong consistency: everyone reads the latest write (often higher latency).
Eventual consistency: reads may lag (better for global scale, feeds, analytics).
For UIs: optimistic updates + reconciliation are common; pair with versioning or CRDTs when conflicts matter.

sections.title: Scaling & load
points: **Load balancers** distribute traffic (L4 vs L7); health checks and sticky sessions when needed.
**Horizontal scaling**: stateless app servers behind LB; session in Redis or JWT.
**CDN** for static assets + edge caching; cache invalidation strategies (TTL, purge, versioned URLs).
**Rate limiting** at edge/gateway to protect origin APIs.

sections.title: Data & caching layers
points: **Browser**: HTTP cache, service workers, IndexedDB for offline.
**Application cache**: Redis/Memcached for hot keys, session, rate-limit counters.
**Database**: SQL for relational integrity; NoSQL for flexible schemas or huge scale (with trade-offs).
**Invalidation**: write-through vs write-back; stale-while-revalidate for read-heavy UIs (SWR/React Query patterns).

sections.title: Async, queues & real-time
points: **Message queues** (Kafka, SQS, RabbitMQ) decouple producers/consumers and absorb spikes.
**WebSockets** or **SSE** for live updates; fall back to polling where needed.
**Idempotency keys** for retried writes; **outbox pattern** for reliable side effects.

sections.title: Frontend-specific architecture
points: **Rendering**: SSR/SSG/ISR vs CSR — SEO, TTFB, personalization, cost.
**Code splitting & bundling**: route-based chunks, lazy routes, Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS).
**Design systems & micro-frontends**: boundaries, shared contracts, independent deploys.
**Observability**: RUM, error tracking, feature flags, safe rollouts.
